stem: Find the flux \( \displaystyle\iint_S \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} \) of the constant vector field \( \mathbf{F} = \left( [[a]], [[b]], [[c]] \right) \) upward through the portion \( S \) of the plane \( z = [[alpha]]x + [[beta]]y \) lying above the rectangle \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) (with upward-pointing normal).
solution: For a graph \( z=g(x,y)=[[alpha]]x+[[beta]]y \) with upward normal, \( \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} = (-Pg_x - Qg_y + R)\,dA = \big(([[c]]) - ([[a]])([[alpha]]) - ([[b]])([[beta]])\big)\,dA \) (a constant here since \( \mathbf{F} \) is constant). Over the rectangle this equals \( \big(([[c]]) - ([[a]])([[alpha]]) - ([[b]])([[beta]])\big)\cdot[[W]]\cdot[[H]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses \( +Pg_x+Qg_y+R \), getting every sign in the normal wrong.
This uses only the \( z \)-component \( R \), as if the surface were horizontal (ignoring the tilt).
This points the normal downward, flipping the sign of the flux.
This is half the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.

stem: Find the flux \( \displaystyle\iint_S \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} \) of \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]xz,\; [[b]]yz,\; x+y \right) \) upward through the flat square \( S \) given by \( z=[[k]] \), \( 0 \le x \le [[W]],\; 0 \le y \le [[H]] \) (with upward-pointing normal).
solution: Since \( S \) is a flat horizontal plane with upward normal \( \mathbf{k} \), the flux reduces to \( \iint_D R(x,y,z)\,dA \), where \( R=x+y \) (the \( x \)- and \( y \)-components of \( \mathbf{F} \), and the height \( z=[[k]] \), do not enter, since only \( R \) contributes on a horizontal surface). So the flux is \( \int_0^{[[W]]}\int_0^{[[H]]} (x+y)\,dy\,dx = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This mistakenly includes the tangential components \( P,Q \) of \( \mathbf{F} \), which never contribute to flux through a horizontal plane with normal \( \mathbf{k} \).
This uses the average value of \( x+y \) over the rectangle's perimeter, rather than integrating it over the full area.
This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This double counts the flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.
This is off by one from the correct flux.

stem: Find the outward flux \( \displaystyle\iint_S \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} \) of \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = \left( [[a]]x,\; [[a]]y,\; [[b]]z \right) \) through the LATERAL (side) surface \( S \) of the cylinder \( x^{2}+y^{2}=[[R]]^{2},\; 0 \le z \le [[H]] \), oriented outward (not including the top or bottom disks).
solution: Parametrize the cylinder's side as \( x=[[R]]\cos\theta,\; y=[[R]]\sin\theta,\; z=z \) for \( 0\le\theta\le 2\pi,\ 0\le z\le[[H]] \). The outward unit normal is \( (\cos\theta,\sin\theta,0) \) and \( dS=[[R]]\,d\theta\,dz \). Since \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n} = ([[a]])[[R]]\cos^{2}\theta + ([[a]])[[R]]\sin^{2}\theta = ([[a]])[[R]] \) (the \( z \)-component drops out on the side, and \( \cos^2\theta+\sin^2\theta=1 \)), the flux is \( \displaystyle\int_0^{[[H]]}\int_0^{2\pi} ([[a]])[[R]]\cdot[[R]]\,d\theta\,dz = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This mistakenly includes the \( \partial_z(bz)=b \) contribution, which only affects flux through the TOP/BOTTOM disks, not the lateral surface.
This uses the cylinder's cross-sectional disk area \( \pi R^2 \) instead of the lateral surface's parametrization.
This is half the correct flux.
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct value.
This subtracts a spurious \( \pi \) from the correct value.

stem: Find the outward flux \( \displaystyle\iint_S \mathbf{F}\cdot d\mathbf{S} \) of the radial field \( \mathbf{F}(x,y,z) = [[k]]\left(x,y,z\right) \) through the sphere \( S \) of radius \( [[R]] \) centered at the origin, oriented outward.
solution: Parametrize the sphere by \( x=[[R]]\sin\phi\cos\theta,\; y=[[R]]\sin\phi\sin\theta,\; z=[[R]]\cos\phi \), with outward normal \( \mathbf{n}=(x,y,z)/[[R]] \) and \( dS=[[R]]^{2}\sin\phi\,d\phi\,d\theta \). Since \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n} = [[k]](x,y,z)\cdot(x,y,z)/[[R]] = [[k]]\cdot[[R]] \) (constant, because \( x^2+y^2+z^2=[[R]]^2 \) on the sphere), the flux is \( \displaystyle\int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{\pi} [[k]]\cdot[[R]]\cdot[[R]]^{2}\sin\phi\,d\phi\,d\theta = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the sign of the flux reversed.
This mistakes the flux integral for the sphere's VOLUME formula \( \tfrac43\pi R^3 \) times \( k \), rather than integrating \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,dS \).
This uses the sphere's surface AREA formula \( 4\pi R^2 \) but drops a needed factor of \( R \) from \( \mathbf{F}\cdot\mathbf{n} \).
This is half the correct flux.
This adds a spurious \( \pi \) to the correct value.
This subtracts a spurious \( \pi \) from the correct value.
